does: This paper provides a systematic mechanistic examination of how six major post-training alignment algorithms (PPO, DPO, SimPO, ORPO, GRPO, and KTO) reshape the internal computations of language models. Using layer-wise linear probing, Sparse Autoencoders, and crosscoders applied across three model families, the authors localize where preference signals emerge in the network and measure geometric transformations in latent space induced by each method. The core finding is that while preference representations consistently concentrate in early-mid or mid-late layers across methods, different objectives produce qualitatively distinct representational changes: KTO and GRPO enhance linear separability through constructive feature sharing and sparse high-salience recruitment, whereas DPO and ORPO degrade separability via non-constructive geometric rotation and feature attenuation, while PPO and SimPO largely preserve baseline geometry. Critically, these effects vary systematically with model architecture.
connects: This work extends a growing line of mechanistic interpretability research that moves beyond black-box evaluation of alignment methods. It builds on prior work applying probing techniques and SAEs to understand learned representations in language models, and responds to questions about what alignment algorithms actually do to model internals versus merely their external behavioral effects. The paper engages with the broader preference-optimization literature spanning DPO, PPO, and newer methods like SimPO and KTO, while grounding analysis in recent advances in mechanistic interpretability tools. It connects to work on representational geometry in neural networks and feature attribution methods, applying these established techniques to the relatively understudied question of how alignment reshapes learned computations rather than just behavioral outputs.
enables: This mechanistic understanding creates a foundation for more principled design of alignment algorithms by revealing the internal mechanisms through which different objectives transform model representations. The findings suggest future work could directly optimize for favorable geometric transformations or feature dynamics rather than treating alignment as a behavioral black box. The architecture-dependent variability discovered indicates that alignment method selection should consider model-specific representational properties, potentially enabling better matching of algorithms to architectures. The insights about feature separability and recruitment patterns could inform new alignment objectives that preserve or enhance interpretability. More broadly, this work enables deeper investigation of whether alignment methods that preserve cleaner internal structure might offer benefits for mechanistic interpretability, scalable oversight, or robustness that are not captured by standard behavioral metrics.
